Mollusca > Bivalvia > Mytilida > > Mytilidae

Little black mussel

Xenostrobus sp.

Updated May 2020

Where seen?

This tiny black mussel is sometimes seen in clusters of many individuals on large boulders at the midwater mark. Grows among oysters, barnacles and other encrusting animals there. Dull greyish ones are sometimes seen in small clusters on mangrove trees and their roots in the mangroves. These may be Xenostrobus cf. atratus .

Features

1-2cm long. The two-part shell is shiny black, thin, fragile and smooth. Those growing on rocks produce byssus threads, sometimes these form a kind of nest in which the tiny mussels are embedded. But the ‘nest’ is not as thick and spongy as the mats created by Nest mussels .

Shiny black ones on a large boulder among oysters. Chek Jawa, Jan 08
Greyish ones on mangrove roots Lim Chu Kang, Aug 05
Pulau Sekudu, Jul 08
Byssus threads form a kind of nest. Chek Jawa, Jan 08
Pulau Ubin, Jan 10

Little black mussels on Singapore shores

  • Photos for free download

Other sightings on Singapore shores

Punggol, Dec 10 Shared by Loh Kok Sheng on flickr .

Links

  • Black mussel ( Xenostrobus sp.) Ng, Peter K. L. & N. Sivasothi, 1999. A Guide to the Mangroves of Singapore II (Animal Diversity) . Singapore Science Centre. 168 pp.
  • Atrate mussel ( Xenostrobus atratus ) from SeaLife Base: Technical fact sheet.

References

  • Tan Siong Kiat and Henrietta P. M. Woo, 2010 Preliminary Checklist of The Molluscs of Singapore (pdf), Raffles Museum of Biodiversity Research, National University of Singapore.
